summary:
The agent states that stimulated saliva contains a higher amount of bicarbonate and other electrolytes like chloride ions, sodium, and hydrogen ions when compared to unstimulated saliva. This difference arises from enhanced salivary gland function triggered by neural stimuli which increases bicarbonate production among others. Key point highlighted here includes an elevated level of bicarbonate specifically attributed to stimulated conditions impacting acid-base balance regulation through altered physiological responses within glands upon activation via nerves or specific agents causing such stimulations directly affecting pH levels indirectly influencing oral health maintenance processes significantly related clinically speaking towards understanding mechanisms behind various dental diseases prevention strategies etc..
